pkg/idempotency.purpose: Single-purpose package that generates UUIDv7 idempotency keys for external API calls (e.g., Stripe). UUIDv7 provides monotonic, time-ordered keys that are safe to use as idempotency tokens without a shared counter.

patterns.name: Key() in production, MustKey() in tests/init only
patterns.description: Key() returns (string, error) and must be used in all application code paths. MustKey() panics on failure and is only appropriate in test setup or package-level init where panics are acceptable.

patterns.name: One key per request — never reuse
patterns.description: Each external API call that requires idempotency must call Key() to produce a fresh UUIDv7. Reusing the same key across requests causes the remote API to deduplicate what should be distinct operations.
patterns.example:
key, err := idempotency.Key()
// pass key to stripe.ChargeCreate or similar

key_file_guides.role: Entire package — exports Key() and MustKey(). uuid.NewV7() is the sole dependency.
key_file_guides.watch_for: uuid.NewV7() can fail in environments with broken entropy sources — always handle the error in production paths. MustKey() panics; never call it inside request handlers.

anti_patterns: Using MustKey() in request-handling code paths — prefer Key() and propagate errors
Reusing the same key across multiple requests — each call to Key() must produce a fresh key
Hand-rolling UUID generation instead of using this package — breaks the centralized key strategy

decisions.decision: UUIDv7 instead of UUIDv4 for idempotency keys
decisions.rationale: UUIDv7 is time-ordered, making keys sortable and cache-friendly in database indexes while still being globally unique — important for Stripe and similar APIs that store idempotency keys in indexed tables.

code_examples.scenario: Generate a fresh idempotency key before a Stripe charge call

pkg/log.purpose: Provides two orthogonal logging utilities: a testify-based mock for slog.Handler used in unit tests, and a PanicLogger recover-and-log helper for goroutine entry points. This is infrastructure glue, not a logger factory — callers must use slog.Default() for actual logging.

patterns.name: PanicLogger via defer with explicit propagation strategy
patterns.description: Always invoke PanicLogger as a deferred call at the top of a goroutine or main, passing exactly one propagation option. The zero value (no option) re-panics — explicitly pass WithExit or WithContinue for workers and servers.
patterns.example:
defer log.PanicLogger(log.WithExit)   // server main
defer log.PanicLogger(log.WithContinue) // worker loop iteration

patterns.name: OTelCodeStackTrace constant for stack trace attribute key
patterns.description: Stack traces must be logged under the OTelCodeStackTrace constant ("code.stacktrace") per OpenTelemetry semantic conventions. Never use a raw string for this key.

patterns.name: Compile-time slog.Handler interface assertion for MockHandler
patterns.description: var _ slog.Handler = &MockHandler{} is declared to enforce interface compliance at compile time. Any new mock in this package must include the same assertion.

key_file_guides.role: Recover-and-log wrapper; exposes PanicLogger and three propagation strategies (WithRePanic, WithExit, WithContinue).
key_file_guides.watch_for: Default strategy (zero value) is RePanic — omitting any option re-panics, which is rarely correct for worker goroutines. Always pass WithExit or WithContinue explicitly.

key_file_guides.role: testify mock for slog.Handler; use only in tests.
key_file_guides.watch_for: MockHandler.WithAttrs and WithGroup return values obtained via type assertion on mock.Called — if the mock expectation is not set up, the assertion panics at test runtime.

anti_patterns: Adding a custom logger constructor here — callers should use slog.Default() or inject slog.Logger externally
Using a raw string instead of OTelCodeStackTrace for stack trace attribute keys
Invoking PanicLogger without defer — it must run inside a recover() scope
Calling PanicLogger with no options in a worker loop — the default re-panic strategy will terminate the worker

decisions.decision: Three named propagation strategies instead of a boolean re-panic flag
decisions.rationale: Workers (WithContinue), long-running servers (WithExit), and test helpers (WithRePanic) have fundamentally different needs; a single boolean would conflate exit-process vs continue-loop semantics.

pkg/lrux.purpose: Generic LRU cache with per-item TTL and a synchronous Fetcher callback, wrapping hashicorp/golang-lru/v2. Primary constraint: all time comparisons use pkg/clock (not time.Now) so tests can freeze time deterministically.

patterns.name: Use clock.Now() never time.Now()
patterns.description: All TTL comparisons inside the package use clock.Now() so tests can freeze time with clock.FreezeTime / clock.SetTime. Any new code added here must follow the same pattern.

patterns.name: Functional options via cacheOptionsFunc
patterns.description: Constructor accepts variadic cacheOptionsFunc. New options must define a func(*cacheOptions) and expose a With* factory function following the existing WithTTL pattern.

patterns.name: Zero TTL means never-expire via zero time.Time
patterns.description: When ttl == 0, ExpiresAt is set to time.Time{} (zero value) via clock.Now().Add(0) logic path. The check item.ExpiresAt.IsZero() short-circuits to cache-hit, meaning ttl=0 is a never-expire signal — not zero-second expiry.
patterns.example: // ttl=0 => ExpiresAt stays zero => item never expires

patterns.name: Fetcher must not be nil — validate at construction
patterns.description: Constructor returns an error if fetcher is nil. Callers must provide a non-nil fetcher; the package does not attempt lazy validation.
patterns.example: if fetcher == nil { return nil, fmt.Errorf("fetcher is required") }

patterns.name: t.Context() in tests, defer clock.UnFreeze()
patterns.description: All tests use t.Context() instead of context.Background() and defer clock.UnFreeze() immediately after clock.FreezeTime to prevent time leakage across parallel tests.
patterns.example:
clock.FreezeTime(baseTime)
defer clock.UnFreeze()
item, err := cache.Get(t.Context(), "key")

key_file_guides.role: Entire package implementation — CacheWithItemTTL struct, Fetcher type, NewCacheWithItemTTL, Get, Refresh, fetchItem.
key_file_guides.watch_for: No mutex around fetch+store: concurrent Gets on an expired key will issue multiple simultaneous fetcher calls. This is intentional for simplicity but must be considered when the fetcher is expensive or has side effects.

key_file_guides.role: Unit tests validating TTL boundary behavior, cache hit/miss transitions, and error propagation from the fetcher.
key_file_guides.watch_for: Tests use clock.FreezeTime with baseTime parsed from RFC3339 — use the same approach for any new TTL boundary tests to keep them deterministic.

anti_patterns: Using time.Now() inside a Fetcher or anywhere in this package — always use clock.Now()
Adding a mutex around Get without profiling — stampede tolerance is an intentional design choice
Constructing with a nil fetcher — the constructor rejects it but upstream callers should validate early
Using context.Background() in tests when t.Context() is available

decisions.decision: Wrap hashicorp/golang-lru with CacheItemWithTTL instead of using lru's built-in expiry
decisions.rationale: lru's native eviction does not integrate with pkg/clock, making TTL boundary tests non-deterministic. Storing ExpiresAt on the item and checking via clock.Now() gives full test control.

code_examples.scenario: Create a cache that fetches string values by string key with a 10-second TTL

pkg/otelx.purpose: Minimal OpenTelemetry span-context serialization helpers for cross-process propagation over non-HTTP transports (e.g. Kafka message headers). Serializes trace.SpanContext to/from base64-encoded JSON using the OTelSpanContextKey constant.

patterns.name: OTelSpanContextKey constant as the canonical header/attribute name
patterns.description: Always use the OTelSpanContextKey constant ("otel.span.context") when storing or reading the serialized span context in message headers or attributes. Never hardcode the string.

patterns.name: Base64-over-JSON encoding for transport-agnostic propagation
patterns.description: SpanContext is marshalled to JSON via trace.SpanContext.MarshalJSON() and then base64-encoded. Reversal uses base64.DecodeString then json.Unmarshal into trace.SpanContextConfig then trace.NewSpanContext.

patterns.name: Nil-check DeserializeSpanContext return value
patterns.description: DeserializeSpanContext returns *trace.SpanContext (pointer). Callers must nil-check before dereferencing even when err == nil, because a non-nil error short-circuits via early return.

key_file_guides.role: Only file; exports SerializeSpanContext, DeserializeSpanContext, and OTelSpanContextKey constant.
key_file_guides.watch_for: DeserializeSpanContext returns *trace.SpanContext — callers must nil-check. The function does not validate that the resulting SpanContext is valid (IsValid()); add that check at the call site if needed.

anti_patterns: Storing the serialized span context under any key other than OTelSpanContextKey
Using W3C TraceContext or B3 propagators here — this package uses its own JSON/base64 scheme for non-HTTP transports
Dereferencing the *trace.SpanContext return from DeserializeSpanContext without a nil check

decisions.decision: Custom base64+JSON serialization instead of standard W3C propagator
decisions.rationale: Kafka message headers carry opaque byte slices; the W3C TraceContext propagator expects an HTTP carrier interface. Base64-over-JSON is self-contained, transport-agnostic, and avoids pulling the full otel propagation API into Kafka message handling code.

pkg/pglockx.purpose: Thin constructor for cirello.io/pglock distributed lock clients backed by a PostgreSQL table (distributed_locks). Enforces the invariant that HeartbeatInterval must be less than half the LeaseTime to prevent lock expiry before renewal.

patterns.name: Validate config before constructing the client
patterns.description: Config.Validate() enforces two rules: LeaseTime >= 2*HeartbeatInterval and Owner must be non-empty. Call Validate() or rely on New() which calls it internally — never pass an unvalidated Config to pglock.UnsafeNew.

patterns.name: Use DefaultHeartbeatInterval and DefaultLeaseTime as safe starting values
patterns.description: The package exports DefaultHeartbeatInterval (3s) and DefaultLeaseTime (1m) which satisfy the heartbeat < lease/2 invariant. Prefer these defaults when no specific timing is required.

patterns.name: Fixed table name — never override per caller
patterns.description: The lock table is hardcoded as the unexported constant lockTable ("distributed_locks"). All lock clients in the codebase share this table — do not create per-feature lock tables.
patterns.example: pglock.WithCustomTable(lockTable) // internal; not configurable by callers

key_file_guides.role: Entire package — Config struct, Validate(), New() constructor wrapping pglock.UnsafeNew with fixed table and configurable lease/heartbeat/owner.
key_file_guides.watch_for: pglock.UnsafeNew is used (not pglock.New) because the safe constructor requires a createTable migration that conflicts with Atlas-managed schema. Do not switch to pglock.New without adding the distributed_locks table to Atlas migrations.

anti_patterns: Setting HeartbeatInterval >= LeaseTime/2 — Validate() rejects this but the invariant must be understood: the heartbeat must renew the lease well before expiry
Leaving Owner empty — the lock client requires an owner string to identify which process holds the lock
Creating per-feature lock tables by overriding WithCustomTable — all locks share the distributed_locks table
Using pglock.New instead of pglock.UnsafeNew without adding the distributed_locks table to Atlas migrations

decisions.rationale: pglock.New attempts to create the lock table at startup, which conflicts with Atlas-managed schema migrations. UnsafeNew skips the auto-create; the table is created via the Atlas migration pipeline.
A single shared table avoids schema proliferation and keeps the Atlas migration surface minimal. All distributed lock use cases in the codebase share the same table with owner-scoped rows.

code_examples.scenario: Construct a pglock client for a billing worker process
